title: Three HPC Gurus Ask: Do We Still Need GPUs?
gist: Three top HPC researchers argue matrix-enhanced CPUs (SVE/AMX/SME + HBM + multi-precision) can make discrete GPUs optional, catalyzed by China's all-CPU 'LineShine' supercomputer topping the Top500; author predicts even Nvidia will eventually embed its GPU tensor cores into an Arm CPU.

insights.claim: HPC researchers Jack Dongarra, Torsten Hoefler, and Satoshi Matsuoka argue in a forthcoming ACM/arXiv paper that discrete GPUs are not fundamentally required once CPUs integrate SVE/AVX vector units, SME/AMX matrix units, HBM, and multiple precision formats.
insights.speaker: Timothy Prickett Morgan
insights.so_what: A credible academic challenge to the GPU-centric AI compute stack could erode the assumption that Nvidia GPUs are a structural necessity for AI/HPC.
insights.quote: The argument is that GPUs are not fundamentally required if the CPU evolves to include the architectural features that made GPUs attractive.
insights.locator: Here is how Dongarra, Hoefler, and Matsuoka see

insights.claim: A CPU equipped with SVE/AVX, SME/AMX, HBM, multiple precision, and matrix-multiply is best understood as a general-purpose processor with accelerator-class numerical machinery, not a conventional CPU.
insights.speaker: Timothy Prickett Morgan
insights.so_what: Reframes the compute market: the value once captured by discrete accelerators can migrate onto the CPU die, changing who captures margin.
insights.quote: It is a general-purpose processor with accelerator-class numerical machinery.
insights.locator: The strongest argument, then

insights.claim: Integrated matrix-acceleration CPUs may be a cleaner platform for tightly coupled AI+HPC workflows (simulation, data assimilation, uncertainty quantification, ML) than host-CPU-plus-discrete-GPU systems that require constant data movement.
insights.speaker: Timothy Prickett Morgan
insights.so_what: The convergence of AI and scientific computing favors architectures that avoid CPU-GPU data shuttling, a structural argument for on-die matrix math.
insights.quote: A CPU with integrated matrix acceleration may be a cleaner platform for this convergence than a system that requires constant movement between a host CPU and a discrete GPU.
insights.locator: This shift could be especially important

insights.claim: Roughly half of Top500 machines remain CPU-only after 15 years of GPU commercialization because GPU offload requires splitting code between parallel (GPU) and serial (CPU) work plus moving data between distinct devices, which is not worth it for many workloads.
insights.speaker: Timothy Prickett Morgan
insights.so_what: GPU adoption has a persistent ceiling tied to programming complexity, not just hardware, capping the total addressable market for accelerators.
insights.quote: which is why only about half of the Top500 machines tends to be accelerated in some fashion
insights.locator: This GPU offload is not for everyone

insights.claim: LineShine, an all-CPU supercomputer using China's LX2 Arm server chip and installed at NSC Shenzhen, is ranked the world's fastest AI/HPC machine on the June 2026 Top500 list and has run trillion-parameter GenAI inference models.
insights.speaker: Timothy Prickett Morgan
insights.so_what: A GPU-free Chinese machine topping the Top500 signals that export controls on advanced GPUs may be routed around via matrix-enhanced CPUs.
insights.locator: a new all-CPU supercomputer called

insights.claim: The LX2 chip is likely fabbed by SMIC on an advanced 7nm node, and its large die size forces it to run at only 1.55 GHz to stay within a 650-watt thermal envelope.
insights.speaker: Timothy Prickett Morgan
insights.so_what: China's process-node handicap (7nm vs TSMC 3nm) is being brute-forced with more silicon and power, revealing the real cost of sanctions.
insights.quote: it can only run at 1.55 GHz to stay in a 650 watt thermal envelope
insights.locator: The LX2 chip is probably etched

insights.claim: LineShine hits 2.2 exaflops on HPL at 52.1 gigaflops/watt burning 42.2 megawatts, versus El Capitan at Lawrence Livermore at 60.9 GF/watt and the most efficient Nvidia Grace-Hopper H100/H200 machines at ~70 GF/watt.
insights.speaker: Timothy Prickett Morgan
insights.so_what: The all-CPU design is currently less power-efficient than GPU systems, so the CPU-only case rests on future process gains, not present efficiency.
insights.quote: the real LineShine is rated at 2.2 exaflops, and if you do the math, that is 52.1 gigaflops per watt
insights.locator: On the High Performance Linpack test

insights.claim: Fabbing the LX2 at TSMC 3nm instead of SMIC 7nm could roughly halve the processor count for the same 2.74 exaflops peak, cut power from 42.2 MW toward 25 MW, and lift efficiency to ~87 gigaflops/watt, surpassing today's best GPU machines.
insights.speaker: Timothy Prickett Morgan
insights.so_what: If China gained 3nm access, an all-CPU design would leapfrog GPU efficiency, underscoring how much of the GPU advantage is process-node driven.
insights.quote: then at 3 nanometers for the LX2 CPU, LineShine would be at around 87 gigaflops per watt
insights.locator: On the High Performance Linpack test

insights.claim: IBM shipped matrix engines in production before any Arm or Xeon CPU, with Power10 (Sept 2021), Power11 (July 2025), and z16/z17 mainframes, and uniquely supports native decimal 'money math' with two digits after the decimal to avoid rounding.
insights.speaker: Timothy Prickett Morgan
insights.so_what: IBM is a first-mover in on-CPU matrix math and positions GenAI inference as a no-GPU workload, a differentiated niche versus Nvidia.
insights.quote: IBM's two families of processors are also the only ones that support native decimals with two digits after the decimal point, which is what I call “money math”
insights.locator: We will point out that IBM Power10

insights.claim: Arm added SVE with Armv8.2-A (Fujitsu A64FX, 2016), SVE2 and the SME matrix unit with Armv9-A (2019), and SME2 with Armv9.4-A (2022); Intel added AMX to Xeon in 2020, first implemented in Sapphire Rapids used in Argonne's Aurora.
insights.speaker: Timothy Prickett Morgan
insights.so_what: The industry-wide march of matrix units into mainstream CPU cores is the enabling trend behind the post-GPU thesis.
insights.locator: The paper’s authors call out the SVE vector extensions

insights.claim: AMD has the capability to add matrix math units to its Epyc cores or package but has so far declined to do so, unlike Arm, Intel, and IBM.
insights.speaker: Timothy Prickett Morgan
insights.so_what: AMD is a laggard on CPU-integrated matrix math, a potential gap or future catalyst depending on how the convergence thesis plays out.
insights.quote: AMD certainly has matrix math units it can add to its Epyc processor cores or within the Epyc chip package, but thus far it has not.
insights.locator: We will point out that IBM Power10

insights.claim: There are three ways to add matrix math inside a server node without a discrete GPU: in every core (Arm, Xeon, Power), on-die outside the cores (IBM Telum/Telum-II mainframes), or on a PCIe card (IBM's Spyre accelerator from IBM Research).
insights.speaker: Timothy Prickett Morgan
insights.so_what: Multiple integration paths exist for matrix acceleration, giving CPU vendors flexibility to displace GPUs without a single approach.
insights.quote: There are three ways to skin this cat without leaving the security perimeter of a server node
insights.locator: The Arm and Xeon designs put matrix units

insights.claim: When GPUs entered HPC in the late 2000s, buyers paid roughly 3X the cost of a CPU-only system for about 3X the performance, so the initial price/performance case was weak and the real early benefit was higher memory bandwidth and lower FP64/FP32 energy.
insights.speaker: Timothy Prickett Morgan
insights.so_what: The GPU value proposition was built over time via software maturation, not intrinsic day-one economics, a reminder that CPU alternatives could follow the same curve.
insights.quote: you paid 3X as much for a GPU accelerated machine to get around 3X the performance compared to a CPU-only system
insights.locator: When GPUs were first coming into the HPC space

insights.claim: Choosing CPU-only machines lets buyers avoid Nvidia's 'CUDA tax' on GPU hardware and the expense of HBM memory, at the cost of lower power efficiency.
insights.speaker: Timothy Prickett Morgan
insights.so_what: Cost pressure on Nvidia's proprietary software/hardware premium is an explicit motivation for CPU-only architectures.
insights.quote: you don’t have to pay Nvidia’s CUDA tax on its GPU hardware, and you don’t even have to go to very expensive HBM memory, either
insights.locator: Yes, they are less power efficient

insights.claim: The author predicts Nvidia will add tensor cores to a future Arm server CPU, and wisely will use the same tensor cores as in its GPUs so the CPU can run the identical CUDA X software stack.
insights.speaker: Timothy Prickett Morgan
insights.so_what: Nvidia co-opting the CPU-matrix trend by extending CUDA to a tensor-enabled Arm CPU would defend its software moat even in a post-discrete-GPU world.
insights.quote: don’t be surprised when Nvidia adds tensor cores to a future Arm server CPU. It is going to happen.
insights.locator: So, given all of this

insights.claim: If agentic AI's balance shifts toward one CPU per one GPU, embedding matrix math directly in the CPU eliminates the rationale for Nvidia's separate-chip 'superchip' offload model (Grace-Hopper, Grace-Blackwell, Vera-Rubin).
insights.speaker: Timothy Prickett Morgan
insights.so_what: A 1:1 CPU:GPU ratio in agentic workloads is the tipping point at which integration beats offload, threatening Nvidia's multi-chip board strategy.
insights.quote: Why have an offload model at all if you can just integrate these functions?
insights.locator: Here is another thing to think about

insights.claim: The author argues future CPUs may warrant 2,048-bit or even 4,096-bit vector engines, a scale a former Intel chief Xeon architect dismissed as crazy.
insights.speaker: Timothy Prickett Morgan
insights.so_what: Aggressive widening of CPU vector engines is a design direction to watch as CPUs absorb GPU-class throughput.
insights.quote: I have argued for 2,048-bit vector engines. Intel’s chief Xeon architect, who is no longer with the company, looked at me like I was crazy. I am beginning to think maybe 4,096-bits
insights.locator: One last thing

insights.claim: Japan's K supercomputer became an all-CPU machine by accident, not design: in May 2009 Hitachi and NEC pulled out of a planned hybrid CPU-plus-vector-accelerator system amid the Great Recession, leaving Fujitsu to build its Sparc64-VIIIfx 'Venus' chip and Tofu interconnect alone.
insights.speaker: Timothy Prickett Morgan
insights.so_what: The most efficient supercomputers historically emerged from all-CPU designs, lending precedent to the current CPU-only revival.
insights.quote: It was not an all-CPU machine by choice.
insights.locator: Here is the funny bit as it relates to the K machine

insights.claim: The 2012 all-CPU K machine remains the most computationally efficient supercomputer across many workloads, and even its successor Fugaku cannot beat it despite a newer Tofu D 6D mesh torus interconnect.
insights.speaker: Timothy Prickett Morgan
insights.so_what: Efficiency does not scale cleanly with machine size, a caution for reading raw exaflops leadership claims.
insights.quote: even Fugaku cannot beat its computational efficiency
insights.locator: The Tofu interconnect that Fujitsu finished

insights.claim: IBM exited being a prime contractor for major US and European national-lab HPC systems because it grew tired of losing money on those deals, and now architects its systems around GenAI inference without GPUs.
insights.speaker: Timothy Prickett Morgan
insights.so_what: IBM's strategic retreat from loss-making HPC contracts explains its GPU-free, inference-focused positioning.
insights.quote: IBM got tired of losing money as a prime contractor for HPC systems for the big national labs
insights.locator: For IBM, GenAI inference is its preferred
